etymology: Coined in 1964 from Greek pétra (stone) + ichōr (the ethereal fluid said to flow in the veins of the gods).
definition: The pleasant, earthy smell produced when rain falls on dry soil.
example: The first storm of summer filled the air with petrichor.

etymology: Coined in 2012 by John Koenig in The Dictionary of Obscure Sorrows; loosely modeled on German sonder- (special) and French sonder (to probe).
definition: The realization that each random passerby is living a life as vivid and complex as your own.
example: Stuck in traffic, she felt a sudden wave of sonder watching commuters drift past in their lit-up cars.

etymology: From Latin de- (out of) + fenestra (window). Coined in reference to the 1618 Defenestration of Prague, which helped trigger the Thirty Years' War.
definition: To throw a person or thing out of a window.
example: Frustrated with the buggy printer, he threatened to defenestrate it.

etymology: From Greek sykophántēs, literally 'fig-revealer' (sykon, fig + phaínein, to show); originally an informer who denounced illegal fig exports in ancient Athens.
definition: A person who flatters or fawns over someone important to gain advantage.
example: The CEO was surrounded by sycophants who agreed with every word.

etymology: From medieval Latin quinta essentia (fifth essence), the heavenly element believed to compose the stars, beyond the four earthly elements of earth, water, fire, and air.
definition: Representing the most perfect or typical example of a quality or class.
example: A trench coat in the rain is the quintessential image of a hardboiled detective.

etymology: From Latin mel (honey) + fluere (to flow). Literally, 'flowing with honey.'
definition: Pleasingly smooth and musical to hear.
example: The narrator's mellifluous voice made even the disclaimer feel like poetry.

etymology: From Greek kakós (bad) + phōnḗ (sound). Sister-word to symphony, which means 'sounding together.'
definition: A harsh, jarring mixture of sounds.
example: The orchestra tuning up sounded like cacophony before the conductor raised the baton.

etymology: Coined by Horace Walpole in a 1754 letter, after the Persian fairy tale The Three Princes of Serendip, whose heroes were 'always making discoveries by accident.'
definition: The occurrence of fortunate or beneficial events by chance.
example: Finding her future co-founder at that random conference was pure serendipity.

etymology: From Latin ubique (everywhere), itself from ubi (where).
definition: Present, appearing, or found everywhere.
example: Smartphones have become so ubiquitous that a quiet train car feels uncanny.

etymology: From Greek ephḗmeros, literally 'lasting only a day' (epi-, on + hēmérā, day). Originally used in medicine for fevers that ran their course in a day.
definition: Lasting for a very short time.
example: Cherry blossoms are loved precisely because they are so ephemeral.

etymology: From Greek alkyṓn (kingfisher). In myth, the kingfisher was said to nest on the sea during seven calm days around the winter solstice—the 'halcyon days.'
definition: Denoting a period of time in the past that was idyllically happy and peaceful.
example: He spoke wistfully of the halcyon days before email was invented.

etymology: From Latin lūgubris (mournful), from lūgēre (to mourn).
definition: Looking or sounding sad and dismal, often exaggeratedly so.
example: He delivered the toast in such a lugubrious tone the bride started laughing.

etymology: From Latin obfuscāre, to darken (ob-, over + fuscus, dark).
definition: To make obscure, unclear, or unintelligible.
example: The contract used legal jargon to obfuscate what was a simple cancellation fee.

etymology: From Latin loquax, from loquī (to speak).
definition: Tending to talk a great deal; talkative.
example: Two cups of coffee turned our usually quiet uncle remarkably loquacious.

etymology: From Latin perniciōsus (destructive), from per- (thoroughly) + nex, necis (death, slaughter).
definition: Having a harmful effect, especially in a gradual or subtle way.
example: The most pernicious lies are the ones that flatter us.

etymology: From Latin sanguineus (of blood), via the medieval theory of the four humors—those with abundant blood were thought to be cheerful and confident.
definition: Optimistic or positive, especially in an apparently bad or difficult situation.
example: Despite the missed quarter, the founder remained sanguine about the long-term outlook.

etymology: From Greek phlégma (inflammation, then 'phlegm'), one of the four medieval humors—an excess was thought to make a person calm and unemotional.
definition: Having an unemotional and stolidly calm disposition.
example: Even with the building alarm blaring, the security guard remained phlegmatic.

etymology: From Latin quotidiānus, from quotīdiē (every day).
definition: Of or occurring every day; ordinary or everyday.
example: He found unexpected beauty in quotidian things—coffee rings, bus tickets, light through blinds.

etymology: From Latin susurrus (a whisper, murmur)—an onomatopoeic word imitating the sound itself.
definition: A whispering, murmuring, or rustling sound.
example: A susurrus of wind moved through the wheat field.

etymology: From Latin ēloquēns, present participle of ēloquī (to speak out), from ex- (out) + loquī (to speak).
definition: Fluent or persuasive in speaking or writing.
example: Her eulogy was so eloquent the room forgot, for a moment, why they had come.

etymology: From Latin recalcitrāre, literally 'to kick back' (re- + calx, heel)—originally said of stubborn mules.
definition: Having an obstinately uncooperative attitude toward authority or discipline.
example: The recalcitrant printer refused every driver update we threw at it.

etymology: From French insouciant, from in- (not) + souciant (worrying), from soucier (to trouble), ultimately from Latin sollicitāre.
definition: Showing a casual lack of concern.
example: He gave an insouciant shrug, as if missing the flight were merely interesting.

etymology: From Latin paene (almost) + ultimus (last). Often misused to mean 'the very last' or 'the ultimate.'
definition: Last but one in a series; second to last.
example: The penultimate chapter sets up a twist no one saw coming.

etymology: From Latin pulchritūdō, from pulcher (beautiful). Famously ugly word for a beautiful idea.
definition: Physical beauty.
example: Renaissance portraits often celebrate the pulchritude of their patrons in flattering light.

etymology: From Medieval Latin saccharum (sugar), ultimately from Sanskrit śarkarā (gravel, ground sugar).
definition: Excessively sweet or sentimental.
example: The film's saccharine ending undermined an otherwise sharp story.

etymology: From Latin truculentus, from trux, trucis (fierce, savage).
definition: Eager or quick to argue or fight; aggressively defiant.
example: A truculent commenter turned every thread into a brawl.

etymology: From Latin ineffābilis, from in- (not) + effābilis (utterable), from effārī (to speak out).
definition: Too great or extreme to be expressed in words.
example: Standing at the edge of the canyon, she felt an ineffable smallness.

etymology: From Latin vicissitūdō, from vicis (a turn, change). Same root as vice versa.
definition: A change of circumstances or fortune, typically one that is unwelcome or unpleasant.
example: She accepted the vicissitudes of startup life with surprising grace.

etymology: From Latin sōliloquium, from sōlus (alone) + loquī (to speak). Coined by Augustine in the 5th century.
definition: An act of speaking one's thoughts aloud, typically alone or regardless of any hearers.
example: Hamlet's most famous soliloquy begins, 'To be, or not to be.'

etymology: From Greek ostrakízein, from óstrakon (potsherd)—in ancient Athens, citizens voted to exile a person by writing the name on a broken piece of pottery.
definition: To exclude someone from a group or society.
example: After the leak, the engineer felt ostracized from his old team.

etymology: From Greek Lakōnikós (Spartan); the Spartans of Laconia were famous for their terse, blunt speech.
definition: Using very few words; concise to the point of seeming rude or mysterious.
example: His laconic 'fine' said more about the meeting than a long debrief could have.

etymology: From Greek apókryphos (hidden away), from apokrýptein (to hide). Originally referred to texts excluded from biblical canon.
definition: Of doubtful authenticity, although widely circulated as true.
example: The story that Einstein failed math is apocryphal.

etymology: From Greek boukolikós, from boukólos (cowherd), from boũs (ox, cow).
definition: Of or relating to the pleasant aspects of the countryside and country life.
example: They traded their loft for a bucolic cottage with a goat that ate everything.

etymology: From Latin cōgēns, present participle of cōgere (to compel, drive together), from co- + agere (to drive).
definition: Clear, logical, and convincing.
example: She made a cogent case for shutting the project down.

etymology: From Greek diaphanḗs (transparent), from diá (through) + phaínein (to show).
definition: Light, delicate, and translucent.
example: Diaphanous curtains turned the morning light gold.

etymology: From Latin effervēscere (to boil up), from ex- + fervēre (to be hot, boil).
definition: Vivacious and enthusiastic; or, giving off bubbles.
example: Her effervescent energy made the dullest meeting feel like a kickoff.

etymology: From Latin furtīvus (stolen), from fūr (thief).
definition: Attempting to avoid notice or attention; secretive, sly.
example: He cast a furtive glance at the exit when his ex walked in.

etymology: From Latin gregārius (belonging to a flock), from grex, gregis (flock, herd).
definition: Fond of company; sociable.
example: Her gregarious nature meant she knew the names of every barista on the block.

etymology: Greek hoi polloí, literally 'the many.' Often misused with 'the' attached, despite hoi already meaning 'the.'
definition: The common people; the masses.
example: He pretended to scorn the hoi polloi while secretly loving daytime television.

etymology: From Greek eikonoklástēs, from eikṓn (image) + klán (to break)—originally a literal destroyer of religious images during the Byzantine controversies.
definition: A person who attacks cherished beliefs or institutions.
example: The iconoclast architect refused to design anything with a right angle.

etymology: From Latin iuxtā (next to) + French poser (to place).
definition: To place or deal with close together for contrasting effect.
example: The exhibit juxtaposes Renaissance portraits with selfies from Instagram.

etymology: Coined in the 20th century from the name of Czech writer Franz Kafka, whose fiction depicts surreal, oppressive bureaucracies.
definition: Having a nightmarishly complex, bizarre, or illogical quality, especially of bureaucracy.
example: Trying to dispute the toll charge turned into a Kafkaesque loop of forms and phone trees.

etymology: From Latin limpidus (clear), possibly related to lympha (water).
definition: Completely clear and transparent; (of prose) easy to understand.
example: Her writing has a limpid quality that makes hard ideas feel obvious.

etymology: From Latin magnus (great) + animus (soul, spirit).
definition: Very generous or forgiving, especially toward a rival or less powerful person.
example: After winning the election he was magnanimous in victory, praising his opponent.

etymology: From Arabic naẓīr (opposite), in the phrase naẓīr as-samt (opposite the zenith).
definition: The lowest point in the fortunes of a person or organization.
example: Losing the contract was the nadir of an already brutal year.

etymology: From Latin onerōsus, from onus (burden).
definition: Involving a great deal of effort, trouble, or difficulty.
example: The compliance review was onerous but unavoidable.

etymology: From Greek panákeia (all-healing), from pan (all) + akeîsthai (to heal). Panacea was the Greek goddess of universal remedy.
definition: A solution or remedy for all difficulties or diseases.
example: More meetings are rarely the panacea managers seem to think they are.

etymology: From Don Quixote, the idealistic hero of Cervantes's 1605 novel, who tilted at windmills he believed were giants.
definition: Extremely idealistic; unrealistic and impractical.
example: His quixotic plan to publish a print magazine in 2025 surprised even his investors.

etymology: French rapprochement, from rapprocher (to bring near again), from re- + approcher.
definition: An establishment or resumption of harmonious relations.
example: The summit marked a quiet rapprochement between the two old rivals.

etymology: German Schaden (harm) + Freude (joy). Adopted into English with no equivalent native word.
definition: Pleasure derived from another person's misfortune.
example: She felt a small, guilty pang of schadenfreude when her rival's startup folded.

etymology: From Anglo-French tant amunter (to amount to as much), from Latin tantum (so much) + montāre (to climb).
definition: Equivalent in seriousness to; virtually the same as.
example: Refusing to answer the question was tantamount to admitting it.

etymology: From Latin umbra (shadow). Once meant 'shade' literally; the modern sense came from the idea of a shadow falling over one's mood.
definition: Offense or annoyance.
example: He took umbrage at being called the 'junior' partner.

etymology: From Latin vērisimilitūdō, from vērus (true) + similis (like).
definition: The appearance of being true or real.
example: The set designer's obsessive detail gave the film a chilling verisimilitude.

etymology: Borrowed from German wandern (to hike) + Lust (desire).
definition: A strong desire to travel.
example: Two weeks home and her wanderlust was already pulling her toward the airport.

etymology: From Greek xénios (hospitable), from xénos (stranger, guest). Same root as xenophobia, but with the opposite spirit.
definition: Hospitable, especially to visiting strangers or foreigners.
example: Their xenial welcome turned a layover into the best part of the trip.

etymology: From Old English gearu (ready, prepared). Once common; now mostly nautical, used of a ship that's quick and easy to handle.
definition: Quick, agile, and responsive (especially of a vessel).
example: The little sailboat was yare in a way the bigger yacht never could be.

etymology: From Greek Zéphyros, the personified west wind in Greek mythology.
definition: A soft, gentle breeze.
example: A zephyr stirred the curtains and lifted the heat of the afternoon.

etymology: Possibly from Middle English in kenebowe ('at a sharp angle'), of obscure origin. Almost always paired with 'arms.'
definition: With hands on the hips and elbows turned outward.
example: She stood arms akimbo, blocking the doorway with her stare.

etymology: From French brouhaha, possibly imitative of crowd noise, possibly from Hebrew bārūkh habbā ('blessed is he who comes').
definition: A noisy and overexcited reaction or response.
example: The whole brouhaha turned out to be over a typo in the press release.

etymology: From Latin crepusculum (twilight).
definition: Relating to or active in twilight.
example: Foxes are crepuscular, slipping through the suburbs just after sunset.

etymology: From Anglo-French demure, possibly from Old French demouré (settled, calm), past participle of demourer (to delay, remain).
definition: Reserved, modest, and shy.
example: Her demure smile in the photo hid the chaos of the day behind it.

etymology: From Greek esōterikós (inner), from esō (within). Originally described teachings reserved for an inner circle of disciples.
definition: Intended for or likely to be understood by only a small number of people with specialized knowledge.
example: His esoteric jokes about category theory landed only with the other mathematicians.

etymology: From Latin fatuus (foolish, silly).
definition: Silly and pointless.
example: The CEO's fatuous platitudes did nothing to calm the room.

etymology: From Latin garrulus, from garrīre (to chatter).
definition: Excessively talkative, especially on trivial matters.
example: Their garrulous neighbor could turn a wave hello into a forty-minute story.

etymology: From Greek hýbris (insolence, outrage). In Greek tragedy, hubris is the pride that invites the gods' punishment.
definition: Excessive pride or self-confidence.
example: Shipping without tests is the kind of engineering hubris that ends in pages at 3 a.m.

etymology: From Latin indelibilis, from in- (not) + dēlēre (to destroy, blot out).
definition: Making marks that cannot be removed; not able to be forgotten.
example: The image of the empty stadium left an indelible impression on her.

etymology: From Latin iēiūnus (fasting, hungry, meager). The 'naive' sense is a later English shift, possibly by association with French jeune (young).
definition: Naive, simplistic, and superficial; lacking nourishment or interest.
example: His jejune take on the war ignored everything that had happened in the last decade.

etymology: From Turkish kısmet, from Arabic qismah (portion, lot). Carries a sense of allotted fate.
definition: Destiny; fate.
example: Bumping into him twice in one week felt like kismet, not coincidence.

etymology: From Latin languidus (faint, weak), from languēre (to be weary).
definition: Displaying a disinclination for physical exertion; relaxed and unhurried.
example: They spent a languid Sunday on the porch, finishing a book between them.

etymology: From Mercury, the Roman messenger god (and the planet), associated with quickness and changeability.
definition: Subject to sudden or unpredictable changes of mood or mind.
example: Working for a mercurial boss meant celebrating the calm days twice as hard.

etymology: From Latin nebulōsus, from nebula (cloud, mist).
definition: In the form of a cloud or haze; vague or ill-defined.
example: The plan was nebulous enough that everyone could imagine it succeeding.

etymology: From Latin obstreperus, from obstrepere (to make a noise against), from ob- + strepere (to roar).
definition: Noisy and difficult to control.
example: An obstreperous toddler had taken over the airport gate.

etymology: From Latin pellucidus, from per- (through) + lūcēre (to shine).
definition: Translucently clear; (of style) easily understood.
example: Her explanation was pellucid; even the interns nodded along.

etymology: Compound of obsolete English quag (marsh) + mire, both meaning boggy ground. The figurative sense came in the 18th century.
definition: An awkward, complex, or hazardous situation.
example: The lawsuit dragged the company into a multi-year quagmire.

etymology: From Latin reticēns, present participle of reticēre (to be silent), from re- + tacēre (to be silent).
definition: Not revealing one's thoughts or feelings readily.
example: He was reticent about his old job, which only made everyone more curious.

etymology: From Latin sublīmis (lofty, exalted), possibly from sub- (up to) + līmen (threshold)—as if approaching a high doorway.
definition: Of such excellence or beauty as to inspire great admiration or awe.
example: The view from the ridge at sunrise was simply sublime.

etymology: From Latin trepidātiō, from trepidāre (to tremble), from trepidus (alarmed).
definition: A feeling of fear or anxiety about something that may happen.
example: She approached the inbox with quiet trepidation after a week off.

etymology: From un- + toward, in the older sense of 'compliant, well-disposed.' So 'not toward' meant unfavorable.
definition: Unexpected and inappropriate or inconvenient.
example: Nothing untoward happened on the trip, unless you count the pigeon in the hotel lobby.

etymology: From Latin vexātiō, from vexāre (to shake, harass).
definition: The state of being annoyed, frustrated, or worried.
example: The endless software update was a small but constant vexation.

etymology: From Old English wynsum, from wynn (joy) + -sum (-some).
definition: Attractive or appealing in a fresh, innocent way.
example: His winsome grin made it impossible to stay annoyed for long.

etymology: From Greek xénos (stranger, foreigner) + -phílos (loving). Counterpart to xenophobe.
definition: A person who is attracted to foreign peoples, cultures, or customs.
example: The xenophile in him kept a running list of countries he hadn't yet eaten in.

etymology: From Old English geon (that), with adverbial suffix. Once standard; now archaic or regional.
definition: At some distance in the direction indicated; over there.
example: He pointed yonder, past the barn, to where the river bent.

etymology: From Arabic samt (path, way), specifically samt ar-ra's (path overhead), via medieval Latin.
definition: The highest point reached by a celestial or other object; the time at which something is most powerful or successful.
example: She published her best book at the zenith of her career, then quietly stopped writing.
